---
title: "Intervals From the Likelihood: Wald, Score, and Profile Intervals"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Statistical Inference"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

rng = np.random.default_rng(20240915)

DATA   = "#2b3a67"     # data / point estimates
CURVE  = "#c1121f"     # the log-likelihood / theoretical curve
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # the MLE / the truth / score interval
WALD   = "#0a7d4d"     # Wald flavour
PROF   = "#7b2cbf"     # profile flavour
SCORE  = "#d6791f"     # score flavour
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded areas
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the third article of the **Interval
> Estimation** movement (C). It assumes [INFR-C1 *Confidence Intervals*], which built the
> **coverage** definition (the interval is random, the parameter is fixed) and the
> **pivotal-quantity** machine that manufactures intervals; [INFR-B3 *Fisher
> Information*], which defined the per-observation information $I_1(\theta)$ as the
> variance of the score and as the negative expected curvature, and the total information
> $I_n(\theta)=nI_1(\theta)$; and [INFR-B4 *MLE Asymptotics*], whose headline result
> $\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,I_1(\theta_0)^{-1})$ is the
> engine of the Wald interval and whose **score** $U_n(\theta)=\ell_n'(\theta)$ is the
> object the Rao interval inverts. From [INFR-B2 *Maximum Likelihood*] it carries the MLE
> $\hat\theta_n=\arg\max_\theta \ell_n(\theta)$ and its **invariance** under
> reparameterization. Nothing new is imported from outside the track. It **sets up**
> [INFR-D3 *The Asymptotic Trinity*] — the three intervals here are the confidence-set
> duals of the three tests there — and it is **consumed directly by the Regression track**,
> which produces every coefficient confidence interval by applying one of these three
> recipes.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

[INFR-C1] built one confidence interval — for a Normal mean — and it leaned on a lucky
accident: a clean pivot $(\bar X-\mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)$ exists in closed form. Most
parameters we estimate by maximum likelihood have no such pivot. This article supplies the
general machinery: three ways to turn the likelihood into a confidence interval for *any*
MLE-estimated parameter. The **Wald** interval $\hat\theta \pm
z_{\alpha/2}/\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)}$ falls straight out of [INFR-B4]'s asymptotic
normality and is the one your software prints by default — fast, symmetric, and sometimes
a lie. The **score (Rao)** interval inverts the score statistic, never plugs the estimate
into the variance, and respects the parameter space. The **profile-likelihood** interval
takes the actual *shape* of the log-likelihood seriously, cutting it at a height fixed by
the chi-square distribution, and is invariant to how you parameterize. We derive all three
from one geometric picture — three different ways to read the same log-likelihood curve —
prove their asymptotic equivalence, and then show, honestly, where Wald fails badly: the
binomial proportion near $0$ or $1$, where Wald can return impossible endpoints and
catastrophically undercover, and where the score-based **Wilson** interval and
**Agresti–Coull** rescue it. The single claim the article defends is that **Wald, score,
and profile are not three competing formulas but three readings of one curve — equal in
the limit, different exactly where the likelihood is skewed — and knowing which to reach
for is knowing how curved your likelihood is.**

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
A confidence interval is a set of parameter values the data find plausible. The
log-likelihood $\ell_n(\theta)$ measures plausibility, so we can read an interval directly
off it — three ways, each a different way of saying "where does the likelihood fall off too
far to believe?"

1. **Wald** — approximate the log-likelihood by a parabola at its peak $\hat\theta$ and
   keep the values within a fixed distance:
   $$
   \hat\theta \;\pm\; z_{\alpha/2}\,\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)^{-1}}\;,\qquad
   I_n(\hat\theta)=n\,I_1(\hat\theta).
   $$
2. **Score (Rao)** — keep the $\theta_0$ at which the slope of the log-likelihood, the
   score $U_n(\theta_0)$, is not too steep relative to the information *at that
   $\theta_0$*; the variance is never frozen at $\hat\theta$.
3. **Profile likelihood** — keep the $\theta_0$ whose maximized log-likelihood is within
   $\tfrac12\chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}$ of the peak:
   $$
   \bigl\{\theta_0 : 2[\ell_n(\hat\theta)-\ell_n(\theta_0)] \le \chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}\bigr\}.
   $$

All three agree as $n\to\infty$. They **disagree exactly when the log-likelihood is not a
symmetric parabola** — skewed likelihoods, boundary parameters, small $n$ — and there Wald
is the one that breaks. The single picture: **Wald trusts a parabola fit at the top; score
and profile use the curve itself.**
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The Wald interval is what you get by trusting that the
log-likelihood is a downward parabola; when that is true (large $n$, interior parameter,
symmetric likelihood) all three coincide and Wald's simplicity wins. The article is the
story of *when the parabola lies* and what to do instead.

# Why we are doing this: the pivot runs out {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **[INFR-C1] gave us an interval for a
Normal mean by finding a clean pivot — what do we do for every parameter that has no clean
pivot?** Every construction below is an answer.

## The problem the pivot left open

[INFR-C1] built its interval on a stroke of luck. For a Normal mean with known variance,
the quantity $Z=(\bar X-\mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)$ is *exactly* standard Normal: the unknown
$\mu$ subtracts off cleanly and what is left depends on no unknown. That is the textbook
definition of a pivot, and rearranging $-z_{\alpha/2}\le Z\le z_{\alpha/2}$ gave the
interval in three lines of algebra.

Now ask for a confidence interval for the **rate** $\lambda$ of a Poisson process, or the
**bias** $p$ of a coin, or the **shape** of a Gamma, or — the case the whole Regression
track needs — a single **coefficient** $\beta_j$ in a logistic regression. None of these
hands you a clean pivot. There is no algebraic combination of the data and the parameter
whose distribution is known and free of the parameter. The exact small-sample distribution
of the MLE is, in general, intractable. The pivot machine of [INFR-C1] simply has no fuel.

What we *do* have, from the Likelihood Spine, is two general facts that hold for *any*
smooth model:

- **[INFR-B4]'s asymptotic normality:** $\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\xrightarrow{d}
  \mathcal N(0,I_1(\theta_0)^{-1})$. This is an *approximate* pivot — approximately
  standard Normal after standardizing — valid for large $n$ regardless of the model.
- **[INFR-B3]'s information** $I_n(\theta)$, which is the curvature of the log-likelihood
  at its peak and, by [INFR-B4], the reciprocal of the MLE's asymptotic variance.

These two facts are enough to build intervals for *anything* we can write a likelihood for.
The price is that the intervals are *asymptotic* — their coverage is exact only in the
limit — and the entire drama of this article is how three different ways of cashing in
those facts behave at finite $n$, where the limit has not yet arrived.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why not just bootstrap everything and skip the likelihood intervals?"* You can, and
[INFR-E2] builds exactly that. But three reasons keep likelihood intervals central. They
are **analytic** — no resampling loop, instant once you have the MLE and information, which
matters when you are fitting thousands of coefficients. They are **what the standard
software reports** (every `summary()` table of standard errors is a Wald interval in
disguise), so you must understand them to read any regression output critically. And the
**profile** interval often *beats* the bootstrap at small $n$ because it uses the exact
likelihood shape rather than an empirical approximation to the sampling distribution. The
honest answer: likelihood intervals when you have a trustworthy model and want speed and
interpretability; bootstrap when the model is shaky or the statistic is exotic.
:::

## A one-paragraph history

The three intervals descend from three statisticians and three eras. **Abraham Wald**
(1943) formalized the test — and hence the interval — built on the asymptotic normality of
the MLE: standardize the estimate by its estimated standard error and compare to a Normal.
**C. R. Rao** (1948) proposed the *score* test, built on the slope of the log-likelihood
at the hypothesized value, which needs the variance only *under the null* and so never
freezes it at the estimate. The **profile likelihood** and the likelihood-ratio interval
trace to **Wilks** (1938), whose theorem — that twice the log-likelihood drop is
asymptotically chi-square — is the cut height we use below. The three are the interval-side
shadows of the three tests of [INFR-D3]; here we build them as *confidence sets* and meet
their testing forms there.

# Intuition before symbols: three readings of one curve {#sec-intuition}

Before any algebra, here is the single picture that makes all three intervals one idea. Draw
the log-likelihood $\ell_n(\theta)$ as a function of the parameter. By [INFR-B2] it peaks at
the MLE $\hat\theta$; by [INFR-B3] its downward curvature at the peak is the information
$I_n(\hat\theta)$ — sharp peak, precise estimate; flat peak, loose estimate. A confidence
interval is a band of $\theta$ values near the peak that the data find plausible. The three
methods are three ways to decide "near."

- **Profile** reads the curve **vertically**: keep every $\theta_0$ whose log-likelihood
  height is within a fixed drop $d$ of the peak. Slice the curve horizontally at height
  $\ell_n(\hat\theta)-d$; the interval is where the curve pokes above the slice. This uses
  the *true shape* of the curve, so if the curve is lopsided the interval is lopsided too.
- **Wald** reads only the **top of the curve**: fit a parabola matching the peak location
  and the peak curvature, then use *that parabola* in place of the real curve. A parabola
  is symmetric, so the Wald interval is always symmetric about $\hat\theta$ — even when the
  real curve is not.
- **Score** reads the **slope away from the peak**: keep every $\theta_0$ at which the
  log-likelihood's slope (the score) is gentle relative to the curvature *there*. It uses
  local first-and-second-order information at each candidate $\theta_0$, not just at the
  peak.

@fig-three-readings draws all three on one skewed log-likelihood. When the curve is a
symmetric parabola the three slices coincide; when it is skewed they peel apart, and the
figure is the entire article in one image.

```{python}
#| label: fig-three-readings
#| fig-cap: 'Three readings of one log-likelihood. The black curve is a deliberately *skewed* log-likelihood (a Poisson rate from a small sample), peaking at the MLE $\hat\theta$ (green). **Profile** (purple) slices horizontally at a drop of $\tfrac12\chi^2_{1,0.95}\approx 1.92$ below the peak and reports where the true curve crosses — an *asymmetric* interval that follows the curve''s lean. **Wald** (green band) replaces the curve with the matching parabola at the peak (dashed) and reports a *symmetric* interval; because the real curve falls faster on the left than the parabola, Wald''s left endpoint is too generous. The mismatch between the dashed parabola and the solid curve is exactly the error Wald commits.'
from scipy import stats

# A small-sample Poisson log-likelihood: lambda from a few counts. Skewed.
counts = np.array([1, 0, 2, 1, 0])         # n = 5 counts, total 4
n_c = len(counts); s = counts.sum()
lam_hat = s / n_c                          # MLE = mean

def loglik(lam):
    # Poisson log-likelihood up to constants: s*log(lam) - n*lam
    return s * np.log(lam) - n_c * lam

lam_grid = np.linspace(0.05, 3.2, 400)
ll = loglik(lam_grid)
ll_peak = loglik(lam_hat)

# Wald: information for Poisson is I_n = n/lam; var(hat) = lam_hat / n.
se_wald = np.sqrt(lam_hat / n_c)
parab = ll_peak - 0.5 * (lam_grid - lam_hat) ** 2 / se_wald**2

# Profile cut height
cut = 0.5 * stats.chi2.ppf(0.95, df=1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.3))
ax.plot(lam_grid, ll, color="black", lw=2.2, label=r"true $\ell_n(\lambda)$")
ax.plot(lam_grid, parab, color=WALD, ls="--", lw=1.6,
        label="Wald parabola at peak")
ax.axhline(ll_peak - cut, color=PROF, lw=1.3, ls=":")
ax.axvline(lam_hat, color=ACCENT, lw=1.4)

# profile interval: where true curve crosses the cut
above = ll >= ll_peak - cut
prof_lo = lam_grid[above][0]; prof_hi = lam_grid[above][-1]
# wald interval
wald_lo = lam_hat - 1.96 * se_wald; wald_hi = lam_hat + 1.96 * se_wald

ax.plot([prof_lo, prof_hi], [ll_peak - cut, ll_peak - cut], color=PROF, lw=3.0,
        solid_capstyle="round", label="profile interval")
ax.plot([wald_lo, wald_hi], [ll_peak - cut - 0.45]*2, color=WALD, lw=3.0,
        solid_capstyle="round", label="Wald interval")
ax.scatter([lam_hat], [ll_peak], color=ACCENT, zorder=5, s=40)
ax.set_ylim(ll_peak - 4.5, ll_peak + 0.4)
ax.set_xlabel(r"rate $\lambda$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"log-likelihood $\ell_n(\lambda)$")
ax.set_title("Wald reads the top; profile reads the whole curve")
ax.legend(loc="lower center", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In one sentence, what is the difference between Wald and likelihood-ratio (profile)
intervals?"* — *"Wald approximates the log-likelihood by the parabola tangent at its peak
and is therefore always symmetric, while the profile interval uses the actual curve and so
follows its skew; they agree when the likelihood is near-quadratic and diverge when it is
not."* The follow-up — *"and the score interval?"* — *"the score interval inverts the
slope-at-the-null test, using the variance under each candidate value rather than at the
estimate, which is why it is the one that fixes the binomial-near-the-boundary
disaster."*
:::

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

We set the stage once. Let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be i.i.d. from a model $f(x\mid\theta)$ with a
single unknown scalar parameter $\theta$ (the multi-parameter case, where the *profile*
interval truly earns its name, is @sec-multiparam). Write the log-likelihood, score, and
information exactly as in the Likelihood Spine:

$$
\ell_n(\theta)=\sum_{i=1}^n \log f(X_i\mid\theta),\qquad
U_n(\theta)=\ell_n'(\theta),\qquad
I_n(\theta)=n\,I_1(\theta),
$$ {#eq-stage}

where $U_n$ is the **score** (the slope of the log-likelihood, [INFR-B2]) and $I_n$ is the
**Fisher information** (the expected downward curvature, [INFR-B3]). The MLE $\hat\theta$
solves $U_n(\hat\theta)=0$ — it sits where the slope is zero, the peak.

We need three facts from the spine, restated so the derivations below are self-contained.
Each is *imported* (derived in the cited article), not reproved here.

::: {#thm-imports}
**Imported facts (from [INFR-B3], [INFR-B4]).** Under the regularity conditions of
[INFR-B4] (smooth log-likelihood, interior true value $\theta_0$, parameter-independent
support):

1. **(Asymptotic normality of the MLE.)**
   $\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,I_1(\theta_0)^{-1})$,
   equivalently $(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\sqrt{I_n(\theta_0)}\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,1)$.
2. **(Asymptotic normality of the score.)** The score at the truth is a sum of $n$ i.i.d.
   mean-zero terms with variance $I_1(\theta_0)$ each, so by the CLT
   $U_n(\theta_0)/\sqrt{I_n(\theta_0)}\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,1)$.
3. **(Wilks.)** $2[\ell_n(\hat\theta_n)-\ell_n(\theta_0)]\xrightarrow{d}\chi^2_1$ at the
   true $\theta_0$.
:::

Fact 1 is the Core result of [INFR-B4]. Fact 2 is the CLT applied to the score, the very
sum [INFR-B4] Taylor-expanded to *prove* Fact 1 — we will re-derive its consequence below
because it is the engine of the score interval. Fact 3 is Wilks' theorem; [INFR-B4]'s
Taylor expansion produces it in two lines, which we reproduce in @sec-profile so the
chi-square cut height is not magic.

## The Wald interval: trust the parabola {#sec-wald}

The Wald interval is the most direct cash-in of Fact 1. From
$(\hat\theta-\theta_0)\sqrt{I_n(\theta_0)}\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,1)$ we have an
*approximate pivot* — call it $W_0=(\hat\theta-\theta_0)\sqrt{I_n(\theta_0)}$. It is a
function of the data ($\hat\theta$) and the parameter ($\theta_0$), and its limiting
distribution, standard Normal, contains no unknown. That is the [INFR-C1] pivot recipe,
applied to an *approximate* pivot.

There is one obstruction: the information $I_n(\theta_0)$ in the pivot depends on the
unknown $\theta_0$. The Wald move is to **plug in the estimate**, replacing $I_n(\theta_0)$
with $I_n(\hat\theta)$. This is legitimate asymptotically: $\hat\theta\xrightarrow{P}
\theta_0$ (consistency, [INFR-B4]) and $I_n$ is continuous, so $I_n(\hat\theta)/I_n(\theta_0)
\xrightarrow{P}1$, and **Slutsky's theorem** ([PROB-21], imported in [INFR-B4]) says
replacing a quantity by something converging to it in probability does not change the
limiting distribution. Hence the *estimated* pivot is still asymptotically standard Normal:

$$
W \;=\; (\hat\theta-\theta_0)\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)} \;\xrightarrow{\ d\ }\;\mathcal N(0,1).
$$ {#eq-wald-pivot}

Now run the [INFR-C1] machine. Trap the pivot in its central $1-\alpha$ mass:
$$
P\bigl(-z_{\alpha/2}\le W \le z_{\alpha/2}\bigr)\to 1-\alpha,
$$
substitute @eq-wald-pivot, and rearrange to isolate $\theta_0$. The algebra is identical to
[INFR-C1]'s, with $\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)}$ in the role of $1/\mathrm{SE}$:
$$
-z_{\alpha/2}\le (\hat\theta-\theta_0)\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)}\le z_{\alpha/2}.
$$
Divide all three parts by $\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)}$, a positive number, so the inequalities
are preserved:
$$
-\frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)}}\le \hat\theta-\theta_0\le
\frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)}}.
$$
Subtract $\hat\theta$ and multiply by $-1$ (flipping the signs, the step everyone forgets,
exactly as in [INFR-C1]):
$$
\hat\theta-\frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)}}\le \theta_0\le
\hat\theta+\frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)}}.
$$
Reading off the endpoints,

$$
\boxed{\;
\Bigl[\;\hat\theta - z_{\alpha/2}\,\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)^{-1}},\;\;
       \hat\theta + z_{\alpha/2}\,\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)^{-1}}\;\Bigr]
\;}
$$ {#eq-wald-ci}

the **Wald interval**. The quantity $\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)^{-1}}=1/\sqrt{nI_1(\hat\theta)}$
is the **estimated standard error** of the MLE — precisely the $\mathrm{SE}$ that [INFR-B4]
derived as the MLE's asymptotic standard deviation. So @eq-wald-ci is just the familiar
$\hat\theta\pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\mathrm{SE}$, and we have now derived where that universal
formula comes from.

::: {#thm-wald}
**Theorem (Wald interval).** Under the regularity conditions of [INFR-B4], the interval
@eq-wald-ci has asymptotic coverage $P_{\theta_0}(\theta_0\in\text{interval})\to 1-\alpha$
as $n\to\infty$.
:::

::: {.proof}
By Fact 1 of @thm-imports and Slutsky's theorem, $W=(\hat\theta-\theta_0)\sqrt{I_n(\hat
\theta)}\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,1)$, derived as @eq-wald-pivot. Hence
$P(-z_{\alpha/2}\le W\le z_{\alpha/2})\to 1-\alpha$ by the definition of $z_{\alpha/2}$ and
the continuity of the Normal CDF. The chain of reversible algebraic operations above shows
the event $\{-z_{\alpha/2}\le W\le z_{\alpha/2}\}$ equals the event
$\{\theta_0\in[\hat\theta\pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)^{-1}}]\}$ pointwise in the
sample space; equal events have equal probability, so the latter also has probability
$\to 1-\alpha$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

**Why "parabola."** The Core called Wald "trust the parabola," and the link is exact.
Taylor-expand the log-likelihood about its peak $\hat\theta$, where the first derivative
$U_n(\hat\theta)=0$ vanishes:
$$
\ell_n(\theta)\approx \ell_n(\hat\theta) + \underbrace{U_n(\hat\theta)}_{=0}(\theta-\hat
\theta) + \tfrac12 \ell_n''(\hat\theta)(\theta-\hat\theta)^2
= \ell_n(\hat\theta) - \tfrac12 J_n(\hat\theta)(\theta-\hat\theta)^2,
$$ {#eq-quad-approx}
where $J_n(\hat\theta)=-\ell_n''(\hat\theta)$ is the **observed information** — the *actual*
downward curvature at the peak, as opposed to its expectation $I_n$. The quadratic
@eq-quad-approx is a downward parabola, symmetric about $\hat\theta$, with curvature
$J_n(\hat\theta)$. The Wald interval is exactly the set of $\theta$ where this parabola
stays within $\tfrac12\chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}=\tfrac12 z_{\alpha/2}^2$ of the peak (since
$\chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}=z_{\alpha/2}^2$, proved in @sec-profile). So Wald *is* the profile
interval, computed on the parabola instead of the real curve — which is why the two agree
exactly when the log-likelihood is quadratic and diverge when it is skewed.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Observed vs. expected information.** Two different denominators both legitimately go in
the Wald formula. The **expected** information $I_n(\hat\theta)=nI_1(\hat\theta)$ uses the
theoretical curvature averaged over the model; the **observed** information
$J_n(\hat\theta)=-\ell_n''(\hat\theta)$ uses the curvature of the *actual* log-likelihood
you fit. They have the same limit (the LLN sends $-\tfrac1n\ell_n''\to I_1$), so both give
asymptotically valid intervals, but they can differ noticeably at finite $n$. Software
mostly reports the observed-information version (it is a free by-product of the Hessian the
optimizer already computed). Efron and Hinkley argued the observed information gives better
conditional coverage. Know that "the standard error" is ambiguous until you say *which*
information, and that the difference is a finite-sample, not asymptotic, effect.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why is the Wald interval always symmetric, and when is that a problem?"* Because it is
$\hat\theta\pm$ a constant — the parabola in @eq-quad-approx is symmetric about its vertex.
That is a problem whenever the parameter has a hard boundary or the true sampling
distribution of $\hat\theta$ is skewed: a symmetric interval for a variance can dip below
$0$, for a probability can exceed $1$, for a rate can go negative — all impossible values.
The Wald interval has no knowledge of the parameter space; it will happily report
nonsense, as we will see for the binomial in @sec-binomial. The fix is an interval built
from a quantity that respects the geometry — the score or profile interval, or a
variance-stabilizing reparameterization (@sec-reparam).
:::

## The score (Rao) interval: never freeze the variance {#sec-score}

The Wald interval's one questionable step was **plugging $\hat\theta$ into the
information**. The score interval avoids it. Start from Fact 2 of @thm-imports, the
asymptotic normality of the score *at the candidate value*:
$$
S(\theta_0)\;=\;\frac{U_n(\theta_0)}{\sqrt{I_n(\theta_0)}}\;\xrightarrow{\ d\ }\;\mathcal N(0,1).
$$ {#eq-score-pivot}

Read this as an approximate pivot. Why does it hold? The score is a sum of $n$ i.i.d. terms
$\partial_\theta \log f(X_i\mid\theta_0)$. [INFR-B3] proved each has **mean zero** (the
expected score vanishes) and **variance $I_1(\theta_0)$** (Fisher information *is* the
variance of the score). So $U_n(\theta_0)=\sum_i(\text{mean-0, var-}I_1)$ has mean $0$ and
variance $nI_1(\theta_0)=I_n(\theta_0)$, and the Central Limit Theorem of [INFR-A2]
standardizes it to $\mathcal N(0,1)$. We have re-derived @eq-score-pivot from scratch — no
import beyond the CLT and the two score facts of [INFR-B3].

Now the crucial contrast with Wald. The information in @eq-score-pivot is evaluated at
$\theta_0$, **the candidate value being tested, not the estimate**. We do *not* plug in
$\hat\theta$. Run the pivot machine: trap $S(\theta_0)$,
$$
P\bigl(-z_{\alpha/2}\le S(\theta_0)\le z_{\alpha/2}\bigr)\to 1-\alpha,
$$
and the confidence set is *every* $\theta_0$ satisfying the inequality:

$$
\boxed{\;
\Bigl\{\theta_0 \;:\; \frac{U_n(\theta_0)^2}{I_n(\theta_0)} \le z_{\alpha/2}^2\Bigr\}
\;=\;
\Bigl\{\theta_0 \;:\; \bigl|U_n(\theta_0)\bigr| \le z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{I_n(\theta_0)}\Bigr\}.
\;}
$$ {#eq-score-ci}

(We squared the two-sided inequality $|S|\le z_{\alpha/2}$ into $S^2\le z_{\alpha/2}^2$; the
squared form $U_n^2/I_n$ is the **score statistic** and reappears as the score *test* in
[INFR-D3].) This is **not** of the form "estimate $\pm$ margin." It is the set of
parameter values at which the log-likelihood's slope, scaled by the local information, is
not too steep — and you generally find its endpoints by solving the boundary equation
$U_n(\theta_0)^2 = z_{\alpha/2}^2\,I_n(\theta_0)$ numerically.

Why is this better near a boundary? Because $I_n(\theta_0)$ is recomputed at each candidate
$\theta_0$, the variance the interval uses *adapts* as $\theta_0$ moves toward the edge of
the parameter space, instead of being frozen at the single value $I_n(\hat\theta)$. For the
binomial this is exactly the difference between the broken Wald interval and the
well-behaved Wilson interval, which we derive in closed form in @sec-binomial — the Wilson
interval *is* the score interval for a proportion.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the practical advantage of the score interval over Wald?"* Two things. First,
**it respects the parameter space** because the variance is evaluated at each candidate
rather than at the estimate, so it cannot run off the end the way Wald does. Second, **it
needs only the null fit** — you never have to compute $\hat\theta$ at all if you only want
to test a value, which matters when the full MLE is expensive or undefined (e.g. a
proportion estimated as exactly $0$, where $\hat p(1-\hat p)=0$ makes the Wald SE collapse
to zero but the score interval is still finite and sensible). The cost is that the
endpoints usually require solving an equation rather than reading off $\pm$.
:::

## The profile-likelihood interval: use the whole curve {#sec-profile}

The third interval takes the log-likelihood's *shape* at face value. By Fact 3 of
@thm-imports, **Wilks' theorem**,
$$
\Lambda(\theta_0)\;=\;2\bigl[\ell_n(\hat\theta)-\ell_n(\theta_0)\bigr]\;\xrightarrow{\ d\ }\;\chi^2_1
\qquad\text{at the true }\theta_0,
$$ {#eq-wilks}
the confidence set is every $\theta_0$ at which this drop is not too large:

$$
\boxed{\;
\Bigl\{\theta_0 \;:\; 2\bigl[\ell_n(\hat\theta)-\ell_n(\theta_0)\bigr] \le \chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}\Bigr\}
\;=\;
\Bigl\{\theta_0 \;:\; \ell_n(\theta_0)\ge \ell_n(\hat\theta)-\tfrac12\chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}\Bigr\},
\;}
$$ {#eq-profile-ci}

where $\chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}$ is the $(1-\alpha)$ quantile of the chi-square with one degree
of freedom. This is the horizontal slice of @fig-three-readings: keep the $\theta_0$ whose
log-likelihood is within $\tfrac12\chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}$ of the peak.

**The cut height, derived.** Where does $\tfrac12\chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}$ come from, and why is
it the *same* as Wald's $\tfrac12 z_{\alpha/2}^2$? Two facts, both proved here.

*First, $\chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}=z_{\alpha/2}^2$.* A chi-square with one degree of freedom is by
definition the distribution of $Z^2$ for $Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ (a standard fact from the
Probability track). So
$$
P(\chi^2_1\le c)=P(Z^2\le c)=P(-\sqrt c\le Z\le \sqrt c)=1-\alpha
\;\Longleftrightarrow\; \sqrt c = z_{\alpha/2},
$$
because $z_{\alpha/2}$ is exactly the point with $\alpha/2$ in each Normal tail. Hence
$c=\chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}=z_{\alpha/2}^2$. For $\alpha=0.05$: $z_{0.025}^2=1.96^2=3.84=\chi^2
_{1,0.95}$, and the half-cut is $1.92$ — the $1.92$ in @fig-three-readings.

*Second, why $2[\ell_n(\hat\theta)-\ell_n(\theta_0)]\xrightarrow{d}\chi^2_1$.* Take the
quadratic approximation @eq-quad-approx and evaluate the drop at $\theta_0$:
$$
2[\ell_n(\hat\theta)-\ell_n(\theta_0)]\approx 2\cdot\tfrac12 J_n(\hat\theta)(\hat\theta-
\theta_0)^2 = J_n(\hat\theta)(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2.
$$
By the LLN, $J_n(\hat\theta)\approx I_n(\theta_0)$ for large $n$, so the drop is
$\approx I_n(\theta_0)(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2 = \bigl[(\hat\theta-\theta_0)\sqrt{I_n(\theta
_0)}\bigr]^2$. The bracket is Fact 1's quantity, asymptotically $\mathcal N(0,1)$, and the
*square* of a standard Normal is $\chi^2_1$ — exactly @eq-wilks. So Wilks' theorem is the
asymptotic-normality result, squared. This is also the precise sense in which **all three
intervals coincide asymptotically**: on the quadratic approximation, the profile drop, the
squared Wald pivot, and the squared score pivot are the *same number*.

::: {#thm-three-equiv}
**Theorem (asymptotic equivalence).** Under the regularity conditions of [INFR-B4], the
Wald @eq-wald-ci, score @eq-score-ci, and profile @eq-profile-ci intervals all have
asymptotic coverage $1-\alpha$, and their endpoints differ by $o_P(n^{-1/2})$ — they
converge to the same interval as $n\to\infty$.
:::

::: {.proof}
On the quadratic approximation @eq-quad-approx, with the LLN replacing $J_n(\hat\theta)$,
$I_n(\hat\theta)$, and $I_n(\theta_0)$ by a common limit $nI_1(\theta_0)(1+o_P(1))$, each
defining inequality reduces to $I_n(\theta_0)(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2\le z_{\alpha/2}^2$. The
Wald interval uses $I_n(\hat\theta)$, the score interval uses $I_n(\theta_0)$, and the
profile interval uses the exact curve, but all three differ from this common quadratic only
through terms that vanish faster than the $n^{-1/2}$ width of the interval. Hence the
endpoints agree to leading order. Coverage $\to 1-\alpha$ for each follows from Facts 1–3 of
@thm-imports respectively. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why is the profile interval invariant to reparameterization but the Wald interval is
not?"* Because the profile interval is defined by the *value* of the log-likelihood, and
the log-likelihood is the same function however you label the parameter — reparameterizing
$\theta\mapsto g(\theta)$ just relabels the horizontal axis, so the set of points within a
fixed height of the peak maps over exactly. The Wald interval, by contrast, is built from
the *curvature at a single point* and a symmetric $\pm$; push it through a nonlinear $g$
and the delta method ([INFR-B4]) only matches the profile interval to first order, so
$g(\text{Wald interval for }\theta)\ne\text{Wald interval for }g(\theta)$ in general. The
classic demonstration: the Wald interval for a variance $\sigma^2$ is *not* the square of
the Wald interval for the standard deviation $\sigma$, but the profile intervals are exact
transforms of each other. This invariance is the deepest reason to prefer the profile
interval when the parameterization is arbitrary.
:::

## The geometry, all together {#sec-geometry}

@fig-three-on-quad puts the three constructions on one figure twice — once on a
near-quadratic (large-$n$) log-likelihood where they coincide, once on a skewed (small-$n$)
one where they peel apart — so the equivalence theorem and its failure are visible side by
side.

```{python}
#| label: fig-three-on-quad
#| fig-cap: "The equivalence theorem and its breakdown. **Left ($n=200$):** a nearly quadratic Poisson log-likelihood; the Wald (green), score (orange), and profile (purple) intervals are visually indistinguishable — @thm-three-equiv in action. **Right ($n=4$):** the same model with a tiny sample makes the log-likelihood visibly skewed; now Wald is symmetric about the MLE and spills too far left, the profile interval leans right following the curve, and the score interval sits between them. The three methods are one method on a parabola and three methods on a skewed curve."
from scipy import stats, optimize

def poisson_intervals(s, n_c, alpha=0.05):
    lam_hat = s / n_c
    z = stats.norm.ppf(1 - alpha / 2)
    # Wald: var = lam_hat / n
    se = np.sqrt(lam_hat / n_c)
    wald = (lam_hat - z * se, lam_hat + z * se)
    # Score: U_n(lam)=s/lam - n; I_n(lam)=n/lam; statistic = U^2/I_n = (s/lam-n)^2 * lam/n
    def score_stat(lam):
        U = s / lam - n_c
        return U**2 * lam / n_c - z**2
    lo_s = optimize.brentq(score_stat, 1e-6, lam_hat)
    hi_s = optimize.brentq(score_stat, lam_hat, lam_hat + 10 * np.sqrt(lam_hat/n_c) + 5)
    score = (lo_s, hi_s)
    # Profile: 2(ll_hat - ll(lam)) = chi2; ll = s log lam - n lam
    cut = 0.5 * stats.chi2.ppf(1 - alpha, 1)
    ll = lambda lam: s * np.log(lam) - n_c * lam
    ll_hat = ll(lam_hat)
    g = lambda lam: (ll_hat - ll(lam)) - cut
    lo_p = optimize.brentq(g, 1e-6, lam_hat)
    hi_p = optimize.brentq(g, lam_hat, lam_hat + 10 * np.sqrt(lam_hat/n_c) + 5)
    prof = (lo_p, hi_p)
    return lam_hat, wald, score, prof

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 4.0))
for ax, (s, n_c, title) in zip(
        axes, [(280, 200, "Large $n$: all three agree"),
               (4, 4, "Small $n$: they peel apart")]):
    lam_hat, wald, score, prof = poisson_intervals(s, n_c)
    ll = lambda lam: s * np.log(lam) - n_c * lam
    lo = max(1e-3, lam_hat - 5*np.sqrt(lam_hat/n_c))
    hi = lam_hat + 6*np.sqrt(lam_hat/n_c)
    grid = np.linspace(lo, hi, 400)
    ax.plot(grid, ll(grid) - ll(lam_hat), color="black", lw=2.0)
    cut = 0.5 * stats.chi2.ppf(0.95, 1)
    ax.axhline(-cut, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.0)
    ax.axvline(lam_hat, color=ACCENT, lw=1.2)
    base = ll(lam_hat)
    for (iv, c, lab, dy) in [(wald, WALD, "Wald", -cut-0.30),
                             (score, SCORE, "score", -cut-0.62),
                             (prof, PROF, "profile", -cut-0.94)]:
        ax.plot(iv, [dy, dy], color=c, lw=3.0, solid_capstyle="round", label=lab)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10.5)
    ax.set_xlabel(r"rate $\lambda$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$\ell_n(\lambda)-\ell_n(\hat\lambda)$")
    ax.set_ylim(-cut-1.25, 0.25)
    ax.legend(loc="lower center", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9, ncol=3)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# The binomial disaster: where Wald fails and score wins {#sec-binomial}

This is the honest edge-of-applicability example the whole article was pointing toward, and
the one [INFR-C1] explicitly deferred to here. The binomial proportion is the most
important one-parameter problem in applied statistics — every conversion rate, defect rate,
poll share, and click-through is a proportion — and it is exactly where the default Wald
interval breaks most spectacularly.

## Deriving all three in closed form {#sec-binomial-derive}

Let $X\sim\text{Binomial}(n,p)$, with $\hat p = X/n$ the MLE. From [INFR-B3] the
per-observation information is $I_1(p)=1/[p(1-p)]$, so $I_n(p)=n/[p(1-p)]$. We can write all
three intervals explicitly.

**Wald.** Plug $\hat p$ into the information: $I_n(\hat p)=n/[\hat p(1-\hat p)]$, so the
standard error is $\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)/n}$ and @eq-wald-ci becomes
$$
\hat p \;\pm\; z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{\hat p(1-\hat p)}{n}}.
$$ {#eq-wald-binom}
Watch the two failure mechanisms already visible in the formula. If $\hat p=0$ or $\hat
p=1$ (the sample is all failures or all successes — common when $p$ is near a boundary or
$n$ is small), the standard error is *exactly zero* and the interval collapses to a single
point $[0,0]$ or $[1,1]$, claiming perfect certainty from no information. And for $\hat p$
small but positive, the symmetric $\pm$ pushes the lower endpoint below $0$, an impossible
probability.

**Score (Wilson).** Now use the information at the candidate $p$, not at $\hat p$. We need
the standardized score $S(p)=U_n(p)/\sqrt{I_n(p)}$ in usable form. Start from the binomial
log-likelihood $\ell_n(p)=X\log p+(n-X)\log(1-p)$ and differentiate term by term, using
$\tfrac{d}{dp}\log p=\tfrac1p$ and $\tfrac{d}{dp}\log(1-p)=-\tfrac1{1-p}$ (chain rule):
$$
U_n(p)=\ell_n'(p)=\frac{X}{p}-\frac{n-X}{1-p}.
$$
Put the two fractions over the common denominator $p(1-p)$ and expand the numerator:
$$
U_n(p)=\frac{X(1-p)-(n-X)p}{p(1-p)}
=\frac{X-Xp-np+Xp}{p(1-p)}
=\frac{X-np}{p(1-p)}
=\frac{n(\hat p-p)}{p(1-p)},
$$
where the last step substitutes $X=n\hat p$ (since $\hat p=X/n$). The information is
$I_n(p)=n/[p(1-p)]$ from [INFR-B3], so dividing by $\sqrt{I_n(p)}$ gives
$$
S(p)=\frac{U_n(p)}{\sqrt{I_n(p)}}
=\frac{n(\hat p-p)/[p(1-p)]}{\sqrt{n/[p(1-p)]}}
=(\hat p-p)\sqrt{\frac{n}{p(1-p)}}
=\frac{\hat p-p}{\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}},
$$
where the third equality cancels one factor of $n/[p(1-p)]$ against its square root.
Setting $S(p)^2\le z_{\alpha/2}^2$ from @eq-score-ci gives the boundary equation
$$
\frac{(\hat p - p)^2}{p(1-p)/n} = z_{\alpha/2}^2.
$$ {#eq-score-binom-eq}
This is a *quadratic in $p$* — the variance $p(1-p)$ now carries $p$, so we genuinely solve
for $p$ rather than reading off $\pm$. Multiply out: $(\hat p-p)^2 n = z^2 p(1-p)$ with
$z=z_{\alpha/2}$. Expanding and collecting terms in $p$ (every step shown):
$$
n(\hat p^2 - 2\hat p\,p + p^2) = z^2 p - z^2 p^2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
(n+z^2)p^2 - (2n\hat p + z^2)p + n\hat p^2 = 0.
$$
The quadratic formula $p=\frac{-b\pm\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}$ with $a=n+z^2$, $b=-(2n\hat
p+z^2)$, $c=n\hat p^2$ yields, after dividing through by $n$ and simplifying, the famous
**Wilson interval**
$$
\boxed{\;
\frac{\hat p + \dfrac{z^2}{2n}}{1+\dfrac{z^2}{n}}
\;\pm\;
\frac{z}{1+\dfrac{z^2}{n}}\sqrt{\frac{\hat p(1-\hat p)}{n}+\frac{z^2}{4n^2}}.
\;}
$$ {#eq-wilson}
Read its structure against Wald. The *center* is not $\hat p$ but $\hat p$ pulled toward
$\tfrac12$ by the term $z^2/(2n)$ — a built-in shrinkage that is largest exactly when $n$
is small or $\hat p$ is extreme. The endpoints can never escape $[0,1]$ (the quadratic was
solved inside the parameter space), and when $\hat p=0$ the interval is still a genuine
nonzero range, not a collapsed point. This is the score interval's "adapt the variance to
the candidate" property paying off in closed form.

**Profile (likelihood-ratio).** Cut the binomial log-likelihood $\ell_n(p)=X\log p +
(n-X)\log(1-p)$ at $\tfrac12\chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}$ below its peak and solve numerically; there
is no closed form, but it is one line of root-finding. It is the gold standard against which
we judge the other two.

**Agresti–Coull.** A beautifully simple approximation to Wilson: add $z^2/2\approx 2$
successes and $z^2/2\approx 2$ failures (for $\alpha=0.05$, "add two successes and two
failures"), forming $\tilde p = (X+z^2/2)/(n+z^2)$, then use the *Wald* formula at $\tilde
p$: $\tilde p \pm z\sqrt{\tilde p(1-\tilde p)/(n+z^2)}$. It is the Wilson center with a
Wald-style symmetric margin — almost as good as Wilson, and easy to remember.

## The coverage catastrophe, simulated {#sec-binomial-sim}

The theory says Wald undercovers near the boundary; the simulation shows *how badly*, and
how completely Wilson and profile fix it. We compute the **exact** coverage of each interval
(no Monte-Carlo noise: for a binomial we can enumerate all $n+1$ possible outcomes and sum
their probabilities) as a function of the true $p$, at a small $n=30$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-binom-coverage
#| fig-cap: "Exact coverage of four 95% intervals for a binomial proportion, $n=30$, computed by enumerating all 31 outcomes (no simulation noise). The Wald interval (red) undercovers savagely as $p$ approaches the boundaries — collapsing toward 26% at the extreme, with violent oscillation from the discreteness — and is the worst performer almost everywhere. The Wilson/score (orange) and profile (purple) intervals hold close to the nominal 95% (dashed) across the whole range, including near 0 and 1. Agresti–Coull (green) tracks Wilson and is slightly conservative. The lesson [INFR-C1] promised: the default Wald interval is the one you should *not* use for a proportion."
from scipy import stats, optimize

z = stats.norm.ppf(0.975)
n = 30
ks = np.arange(n + 1)

def wald_ci(k):
    ph = k / n
    se = np.sqrt(ph * (1 - ph) / n)
    return ph - z * se, ph + z * se

def wilson_ci(k):
    ph = k / n
    denom = 1 + z**2 / n
    center = (ph + z**2 / (2 * n)) / denom
    margin = (z / denom) * np.sqrt(ph * (1 - ph) / n + z**2 / (4 * n**2))
    return center - margin, center + margin

def agresti_ci(k):
    nt = n + z**2
    pt = (k + z**2 / 2) / nt
    se = np.sqrt(pt * (1 - pt) / nt)
    return pt - z * se, pt + z * se

def profile_ci(k):
    if k == 0:
        return 0.0, 1 - (0.05 / 1) ** (1 / n)  # handled below generically anyway
    ph = k / n
    cut = 0.5 * stats.chi2.ppf(0.95, 1)
    ll = lambda p: k * np.log(p) + (n - k) * np.log(1 - p)
    ll_hat = ll(ph) if 0 < ph < 1 else 0.0
    def g(p):
        return (ll_hat - ll(min(max(p, 1e-12), 1 - 1e-12))) - cut
    lo = optimize.brentq(g, 1e-9, ph) if ph > 1e-6 else 0.0
    hi = optimize.brentq(g, ph, 1 - 1e-9) if ph < 1 - 1e-6 else 1.0
    return lo, hi

# Precompute interval endpoints for every outcome k.
intervals = {name: [f(k) for k in ks]
             for name, f in [("Wald", wald_ci), ("Wilson", wilson_ci),
                             ("Agresti", agresti_ci), ("Profile", profile_ci)]}

def exact_coverage(name, p):
    pmf = stats.binom.pmf(ks, n, p)
    cov = 0.0
    for k in ks:
        lo, hi = intervals[name][k]
        if lo <= p <= hi:
            cov += pmf[k]
    return cov

pgrid = np.linspace(0.01, 0.99, 200)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.3))
styles = {"Wald": (CURVE, "-"), "Wilson": (SCORE, "-"),
          "Profile": (PROF, "-"), "Agresti": (ACCENT, "--")}
for name, (c, ls) in styles.items():
    cov = [exact_coverage(name, p) for p in pgrid]
    ax.plot(pgrid, cov, color=c, ls=ls, lw=1.7, label=name)
ax.axhline(0.95, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.3)
ax.set_ylim(0.20, 1.0)
ax.set_xlabel("true proportion $p$"); ax.set_ylabel("exact coverage")
ax.set_title(f"Coverage of 95% binomial intervals ($n={n}$)")
ax.legend(loc="lower center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9, ncol=4)
plt.tight_layout()

# A concrete headline case
k0 = 1
print(f"Observed {k0} success in {n} trials (phat = {k0/n:.3f}):")
for name in ["Wald", "Wilson", "Agresti", "Profile"]:
    lo, hi = intervals[name][k0]
    flag = "  <-- lower endpoint below 0!" if lo < 0 else ""
    print(f"  {name:8s} 95% CI = [{max(lo,0):.4f}, {hi:.4f}]{flag}")
```

The printed headline case makes the failure tangible: with $1$ success in $30$ trials
($\hat p\approx 0.033$), the Wald interval's lower endpoint is **negative** — an impossible
probability — and even the Agresti–Coull interval, being a Wald-style symmetric margin
around a shrunken center, dips a hair below zero here (clip it at $0$). The Wilson and
profile intervals, built by solving inside the parameter space, stay strictly within
$[0,1]$ and are the trustworthy choices at the boundary. The coverage figure shows this is
not a fluke of one outcome: across the whole range of true $p$, Wald's actual coverage
swings catastrophically below its advertised $95\%$ — down to $26\%$ at the extreme — worst
near the boundaries, while the score and profile intervals stay honest near $95\%$.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Never report a Wald interval for a proportion near $0$ or $1$ — and be suspicious of it
anywhere.** Brown, Cai, and DasGupta (2001) titled their landmark paper's verdict bluntly:
the Wald interval is "persistently chaotic" and should not be used; they recommend Wilson
(score) or Agresti–Coull as defaults. The same caution applies to *any* Wald interval whose
parameter has a boundary the symmetric $\pm$ can cross — variances, rates, odds. Whenever
the parameter lives in a bounded or half-line space, reach for the score or profile
interval, or reparameterize to an unbounded scale (next section). The Wald interval is the
default your software prints; defaults are not always right.
:::

## Why reparameterizing helps Wald {#sec-reparam}

There is a cheaper fix than abandoning Wald: change coordinates so the log-likelihood
*becomes* more parabolic, build a symmetric Wald interval there, and transform back. The
profile interval is invariant to this; Wald is not, and that non-invariance is exactly what
we exploit. For a proportion, the standard move is the **log-odds (logit)**
$\eta=\log\frac{p}{1-p}$, which maps $(0,1)$ to the whole real line $(-\infty,\infty)$, so a
symmetric interval in $\eta$ can never produce an impossible $p$. @fig-logit shows the
log-likelihood straightening out under the logit, and the transformed-back Wald interval
landing much closer to Wilson.

```{python}
#| label: fig-logit
#| fig-cap: 'Reparameterization rescues Wald. **Left:** the binomial log-likelihood in $p$ (for 3 successes in 20) is skewed, so the symmetric Wald interval (green) misjudges the curve. **Right:** in the log-odds $\eta=\log[p/(1-p)]$ the same log-likelihood is far more symmetric/parabolic, so a Wald interval built *here* (green) matches the curve well; transformed back to the $p$ scale it nearly coincides with the Wilson/score interval (orange). The trick: Wald works wherever the likelihood is parabolic, and a good reparameterization makes it so.'
from scipy import stats

k, n_l = 3, 20
ph = k / n_l
z = stats.norm.ppf(0.975)

ll_p = lambda p: k*np.log(p) + (n_l-k)*np.log(1-p)
# logit
eta_hat = np.log(ph/(1-ph))
# I_n(eta): for logit, var(eta_hat) = 1/(n p(1-p)); SE on eta scale:
se_eta = np.sqrt(1.0/(n_l*ph*(1-ph)))
eta_lo, eta_hi = eta_hat - z*se_eta, eta_hat + z*se_eta
expit = lambda e: 1/(1+np.exp(-e))
wald_logit = (expit(eta_lo), expit(eta_hi))
# plain wald on p
se_p = np.sqrt(ph*(1-ph)/n_l)
wald_p = (ph - z*se_p, ph + z*se_p)
# wilson
denom = 1 + z**2/n_l
center = (ph + z**2/(2*n_l))/denom
margin = (z/denom)*np.sqrt(ph*(1-ph)/n_l + z**2/(4*n_l**2))
wilson = (center-margin, center+margin)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 4.0))
pg = np.linspace(0.02, 0.55, 300)
axL.plot(pg, ll_p(pg) - ll_p(ph), color="black", lw=2.0)
axL.axvline(ph, color=ACCENT, lw=1.2)
cut = 0.5*stats.chi2.ppf(0.95,1)
axL.axhline(-cut, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.0)
axL.plot(wald_p, [-cut-0.3]*2, color=WALD, lw=3, solid_capstyle="round", label="Wald (on $p$)")
axL.plot(wilson, [-cut-0.55]*2, color=SCORE, lw=3, solid_capstyle="round", label="Wilson")
axL.set_xlabel("$p$"); axL.set_ylabel(r"$\ell_n(p)-\ell_n(\hat p)$")
axL.set_ylim(-cut-1.0, 0.2); axL.set_title("Skewed in $p$", fontsize=10.5)
axL.legend(loc="lower center", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

eg = np.linspace(eta_hat-3, eta_hat+3, 300)
ll_eta = lambda e: ll_p(expit(e))
axR.plot(eg, ll_eta(eg) - ll_eta(eta_hat), color="black", lw=2.0)
axR.axvline(eta_hat, color=ACCENT, lw=1.2)
axR.axhline(-cut, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.0)
axR.plot([eta_lo, eta_hi], [-cut-0.3]*2, color=WALD, lw=3, solid_capstyle="round",
         label="Wald (on logit)")
axR.set_xlabel(r"log-odds $\eta=\log[p/(1-p)]$")
axR.set_ylabel(r"$\ell_n(\eta)-\ell_n(\hat\eta)$")
axR.set_ylim(-cut-1.0, 0.2); axR.set_title("Nearly parabolic in logit", fontsize=10.5)
axR.legend(loc="lower center", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"Wald on p     : [{max(wald_p[0],0):.4f}, {wald_p[1]:.4f}]")
print(f"Wald on logit : [{wald_logit[0]:.4f}, {wald_logit[1]:.4f}]  (back-transformed)")
print(f"Wilson (score): [{wilson[0]:.4f}, {wilson[1]:.4f}]")
```

The transformed-back logit-Wald interval lands far closer to Wilson than the naive Wald did
— and crucially its endpoints are guaranteed to stay in $[0,1]$ because $\eta$ ranges over
the whole line. This is *why* logistic regression reports intervals on the log-odds scale
and exponentiates: the Wald approximation is simply better there. It also explains the
deep connection to [INFR-B4]'s delta method: a variance-stabilizing transform is one chosen
so the delta-method standard error is constant, which is precisely the transform that makes
the log-likelihood most parabolic.

# A second worked example: the Poisson rate, end to end {#sec-poisson-example}

A genuinely common applied problem, carried through all three intervals by hand and code.
A hospital records $X=7$ adverse events over a monitoring period during which the expected
person-time corresponds to $n=10$ "units" of exposure, and we model the count as
$\text{Poisson}(\theta)$ per unit with total $\text{Poisson}(n\theta)$ — we want a $95\%$
interval for the per-unit rate $\theta$. This is small-count data near no boundary trouble,
so it is an honest case where the three intervals *differ modestly* rather than
catastrophically, letting us see the ordering Wald-inside, profile-outside that
@fig-three-readings predicted.

```{python}
#| label: ex-poisson
#| echo: true
from scipy import stats, optimize

X, n = 7, 10
theta_hat = X / n                       # MLE of per-unit rate
z = stats.norm.ppf(0.975)

# Wald: I_n(theta) = n / theta  =>  SE = sqrt(theta_hat / n)
se = np.sqrt(theta_hat / n)
wald = (theta_hat - z*se, theta_hat + z*se)

# Score: (Xhat - theta)^2 / (theta/n) = z^2,  with Xhat = X/n; quadratic in theta
# n*(theta_hat - theta)^2 = z^2 * theta  ->  n theta^2 - (2 n theta_hat + z^2) theta + n theta_hat^2 = 0
a = n; b = -(2*n*theta_hat + z**2); c = n*theta_hat**2
disc = np.sqrt(b**2 - 4*a*c)
score = ((-b - disc)/(2*a), (-b + disc)/(2*a))

# Profile: 2[ell(hat) - ell(theta)] = chi2_{1,.95},  ell = X log(n theta) - n theta
ll = lambda th: X*np.log(n*th) - n*th
cut = 0.5*stats.chi2.ppf(0.95, 1)
g = lambda th: (ll(theta_hat) - ll(th)) - cut
prof = (optimize.brentq(g, 1e-6, theta_hat), optimize.brentq(g, theta_hat, 5))

print(f"MLE per-unit rate theta_hat = X/n = {X}/{n} = {theta_hat:.3f}")
print(f"Wald      95% CI = [{wald[0]:.4f}, {wald[1]:.4f}]  width {wald[1]-wald[0]:.4f}")
print(f"Score     95% CI = [{score[0]:.4f}, {score[1]:.4f}]  width {score[1]-score[0]:.4f}")
print(f"Profile   95% CI = [{prof[0]:.4f}, {prof[1]:.4f}]  width {prof[1]-prof[0]:.4f}")
```

Walk the arithmetic against the formulas:

- The MLE is $\hat\theta = X/n = 7/10 = `{python} f"{7/10:.2f}"`$ events per unit, since for
  a Poisson the MLE of the rate is the count over the exposure ([INFR-B2]).
- The Wald standard error is $\sqrt{\hat\theta/n}=\sqrt{0.7/10}=`{python} f"{np.sqrt(0.07):.4f}"`$,
  because $I_n(\theta)=n/\theta$ gives variance $\theta/n$, so Wald is $0.70 \pm 1.96\times
  `{python} f"{np.sqrt(0.07):.4f}"`$.
- The score interval solves the *quadratic* $n(\hat\theta-\theta)^2=z^2\theta$ — the same
  "adapt the variance to the candidate" move as the binomial — and comes out slightly
  shifted toward larger $\theta$.
- The profile interval cuts the actual Poisson log-likelihood and comes out the most
  asymmetric of the three, leaning right exactly as @fig-three-readings showed a skewed
  likelihood should.

All three are valid $95\%$ intervals; they differ here because $X=7$ is a small count and
the Poisson log-likelihood is mildly skewed. As the exposure grows the three would converge,
per @thm-three-equiv. The honest practitioner reports the profile interval for small counts
and notes the others agree to two decimals once counts are in the dozens.

# The multi-parameter case: what "profile" really means {#sec-multiparam}

So far $\theta$ was a single scalar. The real reason the third interval is called the
**profile** interval appears only when there are **nuisance parameters** — extra unknowns
we must estimate but do not care about. This is the universal situation in regression: we
want an interval for one coefficient $\beta_j$ while $p-1$ other coefficients and a variance
are also unknown.

Let the parameter split as $\theta=(\psi,\lambda)$ where $\psi$ is the scalar of interest
and $\lambda$ collects the nuisance parameters. The **profile log-likelihood** for $\psi$
is the log-likelihood with the nuisance parameters *maximized out* at each fixed $\psi$:
$$
\ell_p(\psi)\;=\;\max_{\lambda}\ \ell_n(\psi,\lambda)
\;=\;\ell_n\bigl(\psi,\hat\lambda(\psi)\bigr),
$$ {#eq-profile-def}
where $\hat\lambda(\psi)$ is the MLE of the nuisance parameters *given* $\psi$ held fixed.
The name is geometric: imagine the full log-likelihood surface over the $(\psi,\lambda)$
plane; for each vertical slice at a fixed $\psi$ you record the *highest* point of the
surface in that slice, tracing the **profile** (the skyline) of the mountain range as seen
from the $\lambda$ axis. @fig-profile-surface draws exactly this.

```{python}
#| label: fig-profile-surface
#| fig-cap: 'What ''profiling'' means with a nuisance parameter. The filled contours are the joint log-likelihood over $(\psi,\lambda)$ for a Normal model: $\psi=\mu$ is the mean of interest, $\lambda=\sigma$ the nuisance standard deviation. For each fixed $\mu$ (vertical line) we slide up and down in $\sigma$ to the highest point of the surface — the green ridge $\hat\sigma(\mu)$. The profile log-likelihood $\ell_p(\mu)$ is the height along that ridge. The profile confidence interval for $\mu$ is where this ridge height stays within $\tfrac12\chi^2_{1,0.95}$ of the global peak (green dot) — collapsing the 2-D problem to the 1-D curve of @sec-profile.'
from scipy import stats

# Normal data; profile mu over nuisance sigma.
data = rng.normal(5.0, 2.0, size=25)
xbar = data.mean(); n_d = len(data)

def joint_ll(mu, sigma):
    return -n_d*np.log(sigma) - np.sum((data - mu)**2)/(2*sigma**2)

mus = np.linspace(xbar-2.0, xbar+2.0, 160)
sigs = np.linspace(1.0, 3.5, 160)
MU, SG = np.meshgrid(mus, sigs)
Z = np.array([[joint_ll(m, s) for m in mus] for s in sigs])

# ridge: for each mu, sigma_hat(mu) = sqrt(mean((x-mu)^2))
sig_ridge = np.array([np.sqrt(np.mean((data-m)**2)) for m in mus])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.4))
cf = ax.contourf(MU, SG, Z, levels=25, cmap="viridis", alpha=0.9)
ax.plot(mus, sig_ridge, color=ACCENT, lw=2.2, label=r"ridge $\hat\sigma(\mu)$")
mu_hat = xbar; sig_hat = np.sqrt(np.mean((data-xbar)**2))
ax.scatter([mu_hat], [sig_hat], color=CURVE, s=55, zorder=5, label="global MLE")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\mu$ (interest)"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$\sigma$ (nuisance)")
ax.set_title("Profiling: maximize out the nuisance at each $\\mu$")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The payoff is that Wilks' theorem still holds for the profile log-likelihood: under
regularity, $2[\ell_p(\hat\psi)-\ell_p(\psi_0)]\xrightarrow{d}\chi^2_1$ where $\hat\psi$ is
the global MLE of $\psi$. So the profile interval @eq-profile-ci works verbatim — cut the
*profile* curve $\ell_p(\psi)$ at $\tfrac12\chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}$ below its peak. This is why
the likelihood-ratio / profile interval is the principled way to get a confidence interval
for one regression coefficient while honestly accounting for all the others, and it is the
construction the Regression track invokes for coefficient intervals.

The Wald analogue in the multi-parameter case replaces the scalar information with the
**inverse of the Fisher information matrix**: the standard error of $\hat\psi$ is the square
root of the $(\psi,\psi)$ entry of $I_n(\hat\theta)^{-1}$. That single diagonal-of-the-
inverse fact — which uses quadratic forms / the Hessian from the Geometry-of-Data track — is
the entire content of "the standard error of a coefficient" in any regression output. We
flag it here as the multi-parameter Wald and develop the matrix machinery where it is
needed; the scalar story above is the heart, and the matrix version is its bookkeeping.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In a regression table, what kind of interval is the reported confidence interval for a
coefficient?"* By default it is a **Wald** interval: the coefficient estimate plus or minus
$z$ (or $t$) times the standard error read off the diagonal of the inverse
information/Hessian. That is why it is symmetric. For a small sample, a near-separable
logistic fit, or a coefficient near a boundary, that Wald interval can be untrustworthy,
and the better choice is the **profile-likelihood** interval (`confint()` in R uses profiling
by default for GLMs; `statsmodels` can compute it). Knowing that the table default is Wald —
and when to override it with profiling — is exactly the practitioner judgment this article
is for.
:::

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

A consolidated, honest map of when each interval is the right tool and when it is not.

- **Wald** is right when the log-likelihood is nearly quadratic: large $n$, an interior
  parameter, a roughly symmetric likelihood. Then it is the cheapest correct option —
  closed form, one Hessian, symmetric and interpretable. It **fails** when the parameter is
  near a boundary (proportions near $0/1$, variances near $0$), when $n$ is small, or when
  a nonlinear transform has skewed the likelihood; symptoms are impossible endpoints and
  undercoverage (@sec-binomial). The first-line fix is often a reparameterization
  (@sec-reparam) before abandoning Wald entirely.

- **Score (Rao)** is the right default when the parameter has a boundary, because it
  evaluates the variance at each candidate and so respects the parameter space; for the
  proportion it *is* the recommended Wilson interval. It needs only the null fit, so it
  works even when the MLE is degenerate (e.g. $\hat p=0$). It **costs** a root-find (no
  general closed form except for nice models like the binomial and Poisson).

- **Profile** is the most accurate at small $n$ and the only one **invariant to
  reparameterization**, and it is the principled interval in the presence of nuisance
  parameters (@sec-multiparam) — the regression workhorse. It **costs** the most
  computation: a maximization at each candidate $\psi$, then a root-find on the resulting
  curve. It still **fails** when the asymptotic chi-square approximation itself is poor —
  boundary parameters where Wilks' theorem degenerates (the limiting distribution becomes a
  chi-square *mixture*, [INFR-B4]'s boundary caveat), or genuinely tiny samples.

- **When to abandon all three for the bootstrap.** When the model is misspecified (so the
  likelihood is wrong and all three inherit the bias), when no tractable likelihood exists,
  or when even the profile chi-square approximation is suspect at the sample size you have,
  the assumption-light alternative is the **bootstrap** interval of [INFR-E2]. The
  likelihood intervals trust the model; the bootstrap trusts the data.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The asymptotics are the catch, for all three.** Wald, score, and profile intervals are
all *asymptotic* — their coverage is exact only as $n\to\infty$. At finite $n$ they
undercover or overcover by amounts that depend on the model's skewness. The ranking is
reliable (profile usually best, score next, Wald worst when the likelihood is skewed) but
none is guaranteed exact for small $n$. When exact coverage matters and the model is
binomial or Poisson, exact methods exist (Clopper–Pearson for the binomial, which inverts
the exact binomial test and is *guaranteed* to cover at least $1-\alpha$ — at the cost of
being conservative). The honest hierarchy: exact when available and you need a guarantee,
profile/score for the best asymptotic behavior, Wald for speed when the likelihood is known
to be well-behaved.
:::

# The duality you will meet again {#sec-duality}

[INFR-C1] planted that a confidence interval and a hypothesis test are two views of one
machine, and here that duality becomes literal three times over. Each interval is the set
of null values a corresponding *test* would not reject at level $\alpha$:

- The **Wald interval** is the set of $\theta_0$ the **Wald test** $((\hat\theta-\theta_0)
  \sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)})^2\le z_{\alpha/2}^2$ fails to reject.
- The **score interval** is the set of $\theta_0$ the **score (Rao) test**
  $U_n(\theta_0)^2/I_n(\theta_0)\le z_{\alpha/2}^2$ fails to reject — read @eq-score-ci
  again, it is exactly that.
- The **profile interval** is the set of $\theta_0$ the **likelihood-ratio test**
  $2[\ell_n(\hat\theta)-\ell_n(\theta_0)]\le\chi^2_{1,1-\alpha}$ fails to reject — that is
  @eq-profile-ci verbatim.

These three tests — Wald, score, likelihood-ratio — are **the asymptotic trinity** of
[INFR-D3], and we have just built their confidence-set duals. Everything proved here about
*when the three intervals disagree* transfers directly to *when the three tests disagree*,
which is the heart of the next testing article. Invert a test, get an interval; that is the
single most useful structural fact in this entire track.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[INFR-C1]**, which built the coverage concept and the pivot machine on
the one lucky case where a clean pivot exists, and explicitly deferred the proportion
disaster to here. This article supplied the general machinery — three ways to turn any
likelihood into an interval — using the asymptotic normality of **[INFR-B4]**, the Fisher
information of **[INFR-B3]**, and the MLE of **[INFR-B2]**. From here:

- **[INFR-D1 *The Logic of Hypothesis Testing*]** — formalizes the test/interval duality of
  @sec-duality into a theorem.
- **[INFR-D3 *The Asymptotic Trinity*]** — the testing side of this article: the Wald,
  score, and likelihood-ratio *tests*, Wilks' theorem in full, and the Neyman–Pearson lemma
  that justifies why likelihood-ratio is optimal. The three intervals here are its three
  confidence-set duals.
- **[INFR-E2 *Bootstrap Confidence Intervals*]** — the assumption-light alternative for
  when the likelihood is wrong, intractable, or the asymptotics have not arrived.
- **The Regression track** consumes this article directly: every coefficient confidence
  interval in a fitted GLM is a Wald interval off the inverse-information diagonal
  (@sec-multiparam) or, better, a profile-likelihood interval. The logit reparameterization
  of @sec-reparam is *why* logistic-regression intervals are reported on the log-odds scale.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The proportion verdict, in full.** L. D. Brown, T. T. Cai, A. DasGupta, "Interval
  Estimation for a Binomial Proportion," *Statistical Science* 16 (2001): the definitive
  case that the Wald interval is "persistently chaotic" and that the Wilson (score) and
  Agresti–Coull intervals are the right defaults — the source of @sec-binomial.
- **The original score test.** C. R. Rao, "Large sample tests of statistical hypotheses
  concerning several parameters with applications to problems of estimation," *Proc. Camb.
  Phil. Soc.* 44 (1948): where the score statistic — and so the score interval — was born.
- **Observed vs. expected information.** B. Efron, D. V. Hinkley, "Assessing the accuracy
  of the maximum likelihood estimator: Observed versus expected Fisher information,"
  *Biometrika* 65 (1978): why the observed information of @sec-wald often gives better
  conditional coverage.
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *The three corresponding tests and Wilks' theorem
  in full* — [INFR-D3]. *Exact (non-asymptotic) intervals* — Clopper–Pearson for the
  binomial, mentioned in @sec-failure, lives in the discrete-distributions corner of the
  Probability track. *Intervals with no usable likelihood at all* — the bootstrap,
  [INFR-E1]–[INFR-E2].
